text: Complete the physiological pathway:
html:
5.

Complete the physiological pathway:

Select your answer:

In the RAAS system, renin converts angiotensinogen into , which is then converted to by ACE primarily in the lungs.

Renin–Angiotensin–Aldosterone System

StepAgentSite of action
1ReninReleased from juxtaglomerular cells (kidney)
2Angiotensin IAngiotensinogen → Angiotensin I (liver)
3ACEConverts Ang I → Ang II (lung endothelium)
4Angiotensin IIVasoconstriction; stimulates aldosterone release
5AldosteroneNa⁺ reabsorption & K⁺ secretion (adrenal cortex → kidney)

Key mnemonic: Renin → Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II → Aldosterone (RAA ladder)

text: Click to highlight the sentence that indicates a contraindication for administering a beta-blocker.
html:
6.

Click to highlight the sentence that indicates a contraindication for administering a beta-blocker.

Select your answer:

Click or drag to highlight sentences

The patient is a 62-year-old female with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes.She currently takes lisinopril and metformin.She has a documented history of severe, uncontrolled asthma with frequent exacerbations.Her current blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg and heart rate is 88 bpm.

Beta-Blockers: Indications & Contraindications

Common agents

Metoprolol · Atenolol · Propranolol · Carvedilol · Bisoprolol

Indications

  • Hypertension
  • Angina pectoris
  • Heart failure (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate)
  • Post-MI cardioprotection
  • Atrial fibrillation rate control

Absolute contraindications

  • Severe reactive airway disease (asthma, COPD)
  • Decompensated heart failure
  • High-degree AV block (without pacemaker)
  • Cardiogenic shock

Relative contraindications

  • Prinzmetal angina (vasospastic)
  • Diabetes with frequent hypoglycemia
  • Peripheral artery disease

text: Read the passage regarding diabetes management and answer the question below.
html:
22.

Read the passage regarding diabetes management and answer the question below.

Select your answer:

AIncreases pancreatic insulin secretion
BDecreases hepatic glucose production
CInhibits carbohydrate absorption in the gut
DIncreases renal glucose excretion
Type 2 diabetes management typically begins with lifestyle modifications including diet and exercise. If glycemic targets are not met, Metformin is widely considered the first-line pharmacological agent due to its efficacy, safety profile, and cardiovascular benefits. It works primarily by decreasing hepatic glucose production and increasing insulin sensitivity. Contraindications include severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30 mL/min/1.73 m2).
